provenance.source_corpus: Trembling aspen root suckering and stump sprouting response to above ground disturbance on a reclaimed boreal oil sands site in Alberta, Canada

source_spans.text: Each selected tree was cut down in May of 2017 at 2.5 cm above the ground.
The probability of root sucker initiation was 7.8 times greater on PMM compared to FFMM
Trees growing on peat-mineral mix were 7.8 times more likely to produce at least one sucker and generally had a higher abundance of suckers.
Tree height was also positively correlated with suckering probability and abundance
larger trees also tended to produce more suckers
competition from surrounding vegetation decreased the probability and abundance of suckering
competition from surrounding vegetation decreased the probability and abundance of suckering
Competing vegetation, however, did reduce sprouting with the probability of a cut aspen to produce a stump sprout dropping by 5% per each 10% increment increase in tree cover
Any cut tree with ≥ 40% surrounding tree cover did not produce any stump sprouts.
Trees growing on peat-mineral mix were 7.8 times more likely to produce at least one sucker
Tree height was also positively correlated with suckering probability and abundance
competition from surrounding vegetation decreased the probability and abundance of suckering
Any cut tree with ≥ 40% surrounding tree cover did not produce any stump sprouts.

edges.subject: causal_mosaic:node:30c0f2ffc6e9
edges.original_sentence: Aspen readily responded to disturbance; however, responses were highly variable between trees, ranging from zero to 47 suckers and zero to 42 sprouts.

source_document.title: Trembling aspen root suckering and stump sprouting response to above ground disturbance on a reclaimed boreal oil sands site in Alberta, Canada

edges.original_sentence: Tree height was also positively correlated with suckering probability and abundance, while competition from surrounding vegetation decreased the probability and abundance of suckering, especially when cover of competing species was greater than 52.5%.
